rationale: AI-Scientist v2 uses literature search during ideation. This placeholder marks ideas as provisionally novel until a separate energy-domain novelty checker is added.

raw.Name: conformal_opf_reserve_activation
raw.Title: Conformal Reliability Envelopes for Secure OPF Under Forecast Shift
raw.Short Hypothesis: Power-system operators do not just need accurate forecasts; they need operationally valid uncertainty sets whose reliability is preserved under changing weather and demand regimes. The hypothesis is that conformal prediction can be adapted to power-system operations to construct bus- and time-coupled uncertainty envelopes for renewables and load, and that embedding these envelopes into security-constrained OPF and reserve activation yields better out-of-sample reliability-cost tradeoffs than Gaussian chance constraints, static robust sets, or forecast-MSE-focused learning. This is the right setting because OPF directly exposes the engineering value of calibrated uncertainty through violations, reserve shortfalls, curtailment, and cost, whereas simpler pure forecasting studies cannot answer whether calibration improvements translate into operational benefit.
raw.Related Work: Prior power-systems work on stochastic, robust, and distributionally robust OPF typically assumes parametric forecast errors, Wasserstein/moment ambiguity sets, or scenario generation, with performance often sensitive to misspecification and regime change. There is also extensive work on probabilistic load and renewable forecasting, including quantile regression and interval prediction, but these are usually evaluated by coverage or pinball loss rather than closed-loop grid operation. Emerging ML work on conformal prediction provides finite-sample coverage under exchangeability, and some recent energy papers use conformal methods for forecasting intervals, but they generally stop at prediction and do not formulate network-aware, security-constrained dispatch rules around conformal uncertainty sets. The key novelty here is to create operational conformal sets tailored to OPF constraints: geographically structured, temporally coupled, and conditionally widened when shift indicators suggest exchangeability breakdown; then to compare them against standard chance-constrained and DRO baselines in both transmission and distribution settings. This is not a trivial extension because the main contribution is the algorithmic interface between calibrated uncertainty envelopes and feasible dispatch/reserve policies, plus the empirical demonstration that predictive validity alone is insufficient unless uncertainty is represented in a network-usable form.
raw.Abstract: Reliable grid operation under renewable uncertainty depends less on point forecast accuracy than on whether forecast uncertainty is represented in a way that remains operationally valid during regime changes. We propose conformal reliability envelopes: data-driven uncertainty sets for load and renewable injections designed specifically for secure optimal power flow. The method begins with any base forecaster for nodal demand and renewable generation, then applies split conformal calibration to produce finite-sample uncertainty envelopes. Unlike standard marginal prediction intervals, the envelopes are structured for power-system use: they are coupled across buses and time, translated into polyhedral or budgeted uncertainty sets compatible with DC or linearized AC OPF, and adaptively widened using simple shift diagnostics based on weather regime, forecast residual drift, or calendar context. These conformal sets are embedded into security-constrained OPF and reserve scheduling so that dispatch decisions explicitly hedge against calibrated forecast uncertainty. We evaluate the approach on transmission and distribution test systems with historical renewable and load traces, comparing against deterministic OPF, Gaussian chance-constrained OPF, and distributionally robust OPF. Metrics include operating cost, empirical violation rate, load shedding, renewable curtailment, reserve deployment, and N-1 feasibility under forecast shift. The central claim is that conformal uncertainty, when made network-aware rather than forecast-only, yields a superior reliability-cost tradeoff and degrades more gracefully under nonstationarity. The project is feasible with standard public datasets, IEEE test feeders/cases, and open-source OPF solvers, and would connect ML uncertainty quantification with deployable power-system operations.
Experiments: Build day-ahead and hour-ahead nodal load/renewable forecasting models on public datasets (e.g., ISO load and wind/solar traces, Pecan Street, NREL, or utility-scale renewable datasets). Compare raw point forecasts, quantile forecasts, and conformalized intervals using coverage, interval width, and conditional coverage across weather/load regimes.

Construct OPF-compatible uncertainty sets from conformal residuals: (i) independent nodal bands, (ii) budgeted polyhedral sets using ranked residual scores, and (iii) temporally coupled trajectory envelopes over multi-period horizons. Embed each into DC-OPF or linearized AC OPF with reserve variables.

Benchmark dispatch policies on IEEE 14/57/118-bus transmission cases and one or more distribution feeders. Baselines: deterministic OPF, scenario-based stochastic OPF, Gaussian chance-constrained OPF, and a standard distributionally robust OPF with moment or Wasserstein ambiguity sets. Evaluate total cost, generator redispatch, reserve procurement, curtailment, overload/voltage violation frequency, and empirical feasibility.

Test distribution shift explicitly by training on one season/weather regime and evaluating on another, and by injecting extreme renewable ramps or load anomalies. Compare whether conformal adaptive widening maintains target violation rates better than fixed chance-constraint confidence levels or DRO ambiguity radii.

Add N-1 screening in a reduced setting: evaluate whether uncertainty-calibrated reserve schedules improve post-contingency feasibility and reduce corrective redispatch. Metrics: fraction of contingencies with feasible recourse, reserve activation energy, and emergency load shedding.

Perform ablations on shift adaptation: no adaptation, residual-drift-triggered widening, and weather-cluster-conditional conformal calibration. Analyze the cost of conservatism versus realized reliability.

For a practical operations study, simulate rolling-horizon dispatch over several months with forecast updates every hour. Measure cumulative operating cost, average and tail reliability metrics, and compute time to verify academic-lab feasibility.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Conformal coverage guarantees weaken under strong nonstationarity or dependence; adaptive widening may become overly conservative.
Translating prediction intervals into joint network uncertainty sets may be approximate and may miss spatial correlation structure important for congestion patterns.
DC or linearized AC OPF may understate nonlinear voltage/security effects unless carefully validated on smaller AC test cases.
Some baselines in power-systems literature are highly tuned; weak implementation could unfairly favor the proposed method, so careful baseline reproduction is essential.
Operational gains may be modest if forecast errors are already well behaved; the strongest benefits likely appear under regime shift, which must be designed credibly rather than artificially.

Full-scale security-constrained AC OPF with multi-period conformal sets may be computationally heavy; the proposal may need to focus on reduced test systems or linearized formulations for the main study.
